behaviors.description: The generated function signature matches the expected interface (parameter names, types, return type).
behaviors.justification: A real failure occurs when either the service parses the LLM output incorrectly, or the validation logic fails to catch a malformed signature. These can be caught by Integration-level tests using mocked LLM responses with various signature formats (correct, malformed, wrong parameters). However, a secondary failure mode—the LLM generating a signature that doesn't match the spec—requires the real LLM (Agentic). Integration is the minimum to test service logic; Agentic is needed to catch real LLM generation failures.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return various function code samples: correct signatures matching the spec, malformed syntax, wrong parameter names, and missing/extra parameters. Run these through the service's parsing and validation logic. Verify it correctly accepts spec-compliant signatures and rejects (or flags) non-compliant ones.

behaviors.description: The generated implementation passes all provided test cases on the first or a subsequent attempt.
behaviors.justification: To verify generated code actually works, you must execute it against the test suite. Mocking the test execution would hide real failures: the code might have subtle bugs that only surface when run in a real runtime environment. System-level testing executes the generated code in a real subprocess/runtime where actual test frameworks run. This is the only way to catch real execution failures (runtime errors, logic bugs, concurrency issues).
behaviors.test_description: Generate code via LLM for a function with a provided test suite. Execute the generated code against the test cases in a real runtime environment (subprocess, interpreter, or container). Verify tests pass on the first attempt if code is correct. For cases where initial code fails, verify that after refinement iterations, tests eventually pass.

behaviors.description: When tests fail, the refinement step includes the failure messages in the next LLM prompt so the model can learn from its mistakes.
behaviors.justification: This behavior tests prompt construction logic—verifying that the service correctly assembles the next LLM call to include test failure details. This is a pure logic test: given failure messages, does the prompt include them? You can mock both the LLM calls (to capture what prompt is sent) and the test output (to provide predetermined failure messages). This tests the service implementation, not the LLM's ability to use feedback. Integration-level tests would use real test failures, improving confidence in realism.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to return code that fails tests. Provide mocked test failure output (specific assertion failures, error messages). Trigger the refinement loop. Capture the next LLM prompt and verify it includes the failure details (stack traces, failed assertions, test names) so the LLM has the context to improve.

behaviors.description: The service stops after 3 failed attempts and returns a structured error with all three attempts and their test results.
behaviors.justification: This is a control-flow and response-structure test. The service must track attempt count, halt after the 3rd failure, and format an error response. None of this depends on LLM quality, test execution, or external systems—it's deterministic logic that can be fully tested by mocking the LLM to consistently return failing code and verifying the service stops and returns the correct error structure.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the LLM to generate failing code consistently. Run the service with this mock and verify it stops after exactly 3 refinement attempts (4 total attempts: 1 initial generation + 3 refinements). Verify the returned error structure contains all 4 attempts' code samples, test results, and failure messages.

behaviors.description: The full loop—generate, test, refine, test again—produces a working implementation for a function that a single LLM call typically gets wrong.
behaviors.justification: This is an end-to-end behavior that tests whether the iterative refinement strategy actually improves code quality. For a 'hard' function that requires iteration, you cannot use mocks; mocking the LLM with hardcoded 'correct' code would pass trivially. You need real LLM calls to verify that (1) the initial attempt fails as expected, (2) the feedback loop works, and (3) refinement eventually produces working code. This requires Agentic-level testing. Workflow is also relevant since multiple LLM calls happen in sequence, but Agentic is the minimum to catch real failures.
behaviors.test_description: Select a function specification known to be challenging for a single LLM call (e.g., complex algorithm, non-obvious edge cases). Execute the full service loop with a real LLM. Track all refinement iterations. Verify that the final generated code passes all provided test cases. Repeat with multiple difficult specs to ensure robustness.
